channel.title: MiSTerZine
channel.description: A collection of quotes from around the web on MiSTer FPGA releases, each with its source linked.

item.title: Breakout (50th anniversary)
item.description:
"The reason Atari wanted me to design it is they were tired of their games taking 150, 200 chips, and they knew I designed things with very few chips, so we had incentives for getting it under 50 or under 40 chips," Steve Wozniak said of Breakout in a 2007 interview with Gamasutra. Atari's engineers, faced with the finished board: "They had trouble understanding it."

MiSTer debut 2020-12-12.

item.title: Night Slashers (MiSTer debut 2026-07-10)
item.description: "Data East also created Night Slashers, clearly their best beat-em-up and undoubtedly capable of cracking a connoisseur's Top 10 list, if it weren't for the game's unbelievable obscurity," reads a 2006 review at HonestGamers, which sums up the appeal: "watch the heads roll, the chests implode, and the guts soil the cement."

item.title: Trio The Punch (MiSTer debut 2026-07-08)
item.description: "Nothing about this game is done well, and yet everything just meshes together so perfectly into one gigantic dose of pure nonsense," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, which also says Trio The Punch "is like a visual representation of the writings of William S. Burroughs if he was a Japanese video game designer."

item.title: Battle Garegga (30th anniversary)
item.description:
"A number of shoot-em-up fans regard it as one of the best shooters of all time; others feel that the cluttered visual style and rank system ruin some pretty cool ideas," writes Hardcore Gaming 101. That rank system reacts to how well you play, and "creates some ridiculous scenarios where players will intentionally suicide at points to make the difficulty manageable, and can only properly clear it by scoring high enough to rack up extra lives."

MiSTer debut 2023-09-10.

item.title: Cabal (MiSTer debut 2026-07-05)
item.description: "The rather obscure Japanese TAD Corporation wondered what it would be like to give a third-person point of view to gun-based carnage," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, crediting Cabal with the wave of imitators that followed, Wild Guns and NAM-1975 among them: "None of these games would likely exist if Cabal didn't set the table."

item.title: Biomechanical Toy (MiSTer debut 2026-07-05)
item.description: "The colorful, trippy graphics are reminiscent of many Amiga games," writes Hardcore Gaming 101 of Spanish maker Gaelco's run-and-gun. "It's not really on the level of other genre greats like Metal Slug (though it did predate that game by about a year)."

item.title: Flash Boy (MiSTer debut 2026-06-30)
item.description: Flash Boy shipped on Data East's DECO Cassette System, tape-loaded arcade hardware where "the machine was turned on and 2 to 3 minutes later the game was loaded and ready to be played," as AtariProtos describes it. "The tape medium didn't prove to be nearly as robust as Data East had hoped, and the cassettes had a bad habit of failing to load after only a few months."

item.title: Darius II (MiSTer debut 2026-06-16)
item.description: "In addition to a three-screen release, there is also a (more common) two-screen version," notes Hardcore Gaming 101; the MiSTer core runs the two-screen one. "For some unknown reason, Darius II was renamed Sagaia outside of Japan."
